disease.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis
disease.note: Chronic progressive hepatitis characterized by interface hepatitis, autoantibodies, hypergammaglobulinemia, and response to immunosuppression. Bimodal age distribution: first peak in children/young adults (10-30 years), second peak in adults (40-60 years).

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute severe or fulminant -> EMERGENCY UCU; Active moderate -> URGENT immunosuppression initiation; Asymptomatic mild -> OUTPATIENT management

historyFeatures.note: Autoimmune thyroiditis, RA, Sjogren's, IBD, celiac, type 1 DM
In women, associated with disease activity

examFeatures.note: Spider angiomas, palmar erythema in chronic advanced disease

subtypes.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis Type 1
subtypes.note: Most common form (80%). Positive ANA and/or anti-smooth muscle antibody (anti-actin). Peak in young women but can present at any age. HLA-DR3 (younger, more severe) and HLA-DR4 (older, milder) associated.

subtypes.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis Type 2
subtypes.note: Anti-liver kidney microsomal type 1 antibody (anti-LKM1) positive, anti-LC1 positive. Primarily affects children (peak 2-14 years). More severe presentation, higher rate of acute liver failure. More common in Europe. Associated with IgA deficiency and other autoimmune syndromes.

subtypes.name: Autoimmune Hepatitis (Acute Severe)
subtypes.note: Presents with acute hepatitis-like symptoms, INR >1.5, severe jaundice. May progress to acute liver failure. Differentiating from DILI is critical. High-dose steroids first-line.

subtypes.name: AIH Variant/Overlap Syndromes
subtypes.note: Overlap features with PBC (AMA+, cholestatic LFTs) or PSC (cholangiographic changes). Treated based on predominant pattern.

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin total/direct)
laboratory.indication: ALT/AST typically elevated (hepatitic pattern); ALP/GGT may be mildly elevated (if <3x ULN, favors AIH; if >3x ULN, consider overlap/PSC/PBC)

laboratory.name: Serum IgG level (total immunoglobulins)
laboratory.indication: elevated IgG (typically 1.2-2x ULN) is a key diagnostic feature; part of AIH score

laboratory.name: Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA)
laboratory.indication: positive in 75% of type 1 AIH; titer >=1:40 considered positive

laboratory.name: Anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA, anti-actin)
laboratory.indication: more specific for AIH than ANA; titer >=1:40 positive; anti-F-actin more specific

laboratory.name: Anti-liver kidney microsomal type 1 antibody (anti-LKM-1)
laboratory.indication: positive in type 2 AIH (children); target CYP2D6

laboratory.name: Anti-liver cytosol type 1 antibody (anti-LC-1)
laboratory.indication: positive in some type 2 AIH cases

laboratory.name: Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA)
laboratory.indication: typically negative; positive suggests PBC overlap

laboratory.name: Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA, p-ANCA)
laboratory.indication: often positive in AIH (atypical p-ANCA) but non-specific

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; prolonged INR indicates severe disease

laboratory.name: Viral hepatitis serology (HAV, HBV, HCV, HEV, EBV, CMV)
Ceruloplasmin, 24h urine copper
laboratory.indication: rule out Wilson's if young age or neuropsychiatric symptoms

laboratory.name: AIH Simplified Diagnostic Score (if uncertain)
laboratory.indication: uses autoantibodies, IgG, histology, viral exclusion. Score >=6 probable; >=7 definite

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: hepatomegaly, cirrhosis assessment, HCC screening, exclude biliary obstruction

imaging.name: Transient elastography (FibroScan)
imaging.indication: fibrosis stage at diagnosis and during follow-up

imaging.name: MRCP (if cholestatic pattern)
imaging.indication: rule out PSC if ALP/GGT markedly elevated

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: essential for diagnosis: interface hepatitis, plasma cell infiltration, rosetting, emperipolesis. Exclude other diseases. Stage fibrosis. Must be done before treatment initiation if safe and feasible.

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Autoimmune Hepatitis; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Autoimmune Hepatitis

pathways.criteria: ALT <10x ULN, no coagulopathy, no encephalopathy, able to take oral medications
pathways.treatment: Prednisolone 30-40mg PO daily for 2-4 weeks, then taper over 4-6 weeks to maintenance of 5-10mg/day (based on symptoms and ALT). Add azathioprine 1-2mg/kg PO daily (start after 2-4 weeks or when bilirubin <6mg/dL). Target: ALT normalization or near-normalization. Alternative: budesonide 9mg PO daily (preferred in non-cirrhotic, less Cushingoid side effects) + azathioprine. Monitor LFTs q1-2 weeks initially, then monthly. Continue for minimum 2 years after biochemical remission before considering withdrawal (relapse risk 50-80% if withdrawn).

pathways.criteria: ALT >10x ULN, INR >1.5, marked jaundice (bilirubin >5-10mg/dL), no encephalopathy
pathways.treatment: Prednisolone 40-60mg PO daily or methylprednisolone 40-60mg IV daily. Taper based on response. Monitor ALT, bilirubin, INR daily. If improving in 5-7 days: transition to oral pred and taper. If no improvement in 5-7 days with INR or bilirubin worsening: consider second-line agents and transplant evaluation. Azathioprine can be added when bilirubin <6mg/dL. If contraindications to azathioprine: mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 500-1000mg PO BID.

Urgent transplant center referral and listing. High-dose methylprednisolone 60mg IV daily x 3-5 days as trial; but if no biochemical improvement, do NOT delay transplantation. NAC may be considered (limited evidence). If listed: continue steroids until transplant. Bridging to transplant with ICU support. Liver biopsy via transjugular route if needed (coagulopathy). Steroid non-response in fulminant AIH has high mortality; early listing is critical.

pathways.criteria: Failure to achieve remission on prednisolone + azathioprine after 6-12 months (ALT persistently elevated, IgG elevated)
pathways.treatment: Optimize adherence and dosing (check azathioprine metabolites: 6-TGN 230-450 pmol/8x10^8 RBC; 6-MMP <5700). Switch azathioprine to MMF 500-1000mg PO BID. Alternative: calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.05-0.1mg/kg PO divided BID, target trough 3-6 ng/mL). Alternative: cyclosporine, sirolimus. Anti-TNF (infliximab) in extreme cases (with caution: risk of infection). Repeat liver biopsy to assess disease activity and fibrosis. Assess compliance: 40% of 'non-responders' have poor adherence.

pathways.criteria: AIH patient planning pregnancy or pregnant
pathways.treatment: Pre-pregnancy: achieve stable remission before conception. During pregnancy: continue prednisolone (lowest effective dose, typically 5-10mg/day) and azathioprine (lowest effective dose 50-100mg/day; meta-analyses show safety in pregnancy). Budesonide has limited fetal safety data. Monitor ALT every 4-6 weeks during pregnancy and 8-12 weeks postpartum (flare risk highest postpartum). MMF is contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic); switch to azathioprine before conception.

disease.emergencyPriority: high for acute severe/fulminant; medium for moderate; low for mild
